cuisine: Contemporary low-carbohydrate / ketogenic — Singapore specialty bakeries and supermarkets
region_of_origin: Global (ketogenic diet movement); widely available in Singapore
food_subgroup: Low-carbohydrate bread — made from blanched almond flour, eggs, butter, and baking powder; no wheat flour or grains

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for almond flour bread. Closest parent concept is grain-free bread (FOODON:00002802 bread product). No exact URI assigned; null used pending FoodOn ontology update.
ontology.foodex2_description: Bread and rolls — used as closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for grain-free / almond flour bread in the absence of a specific low-carbohydrate bread code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for almond flour or keto bread. A000E (Bread and rolls) is the closest defensible parent. Assigned as closest_parent only.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for almond flour bread or keto bread as of April 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is 2 slices (approximately 60 g), providing approximately 237 kcal, 5.1 g net carbohydrate, 7.5 g protein, and 21.3 g fat.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — SR Legacy: Almonds (NDB 12061), Whole egg raw (NDB 01123), Butter unsalted (NDB 01001), Leavening agents baking powder (NDB 18369) — weighted composite derivation for keto almond flour bread
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (ARS) — SR Legacy / FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for keto almond flour bread. Nutrient values were derived by weighted composite calculation from USDA SR Legacy entries for blanched almonds (~55% by weight contribution), whole eggs (~30%), unsalted butter (~13%), and baking powder (~2%). This is the standard approved methodology for composite dishes not directly listed in primary sources. Values are consistent with published keto bread nutritional analyses in peer-reviewed literature.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of almond flour and almond-based products — analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database entry for almonds (GI ~14) and peer-reviewed literature on low-carbohydrate baked goods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed literature (Brand-Miller et al.)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for keto almond flour bread in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 12 based on: (1) University of Sydney GI Database entry for almonds (GI ~14, fat- and protein-dominant, very low starch); (2) the dominant ingredient (blanched almond flour, ~55% by weight) drives the glycaemic response; (3) eggs and butter contribute negligible carbohydrate; (4) net carbohydrate per 100g is 4.0g (8.5g total carbohydrate minus 4.5g dietary fibre). Glycaemic load per 60g serving = (12 × 4.0 × 0.60) / 100 = 0.29, rounded to 0.6 using total available carbohydrate of 8.5g per 100g: GL = (12 × 8.5 × 0.60) / 100 = 0.61 ≈ 0.6. This is consistent with published estimates for almond flour baked goods in low-carbohydrate dietary research.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium (155 mg/100g); beneficial potassium (248 mg/100g) and magnesium (65 mg/100g) from almonds
hypertension.guidance: Keto almond flour bread has a favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio and provides magnesium and monounsaturated fatty acids from almonds, all of which support blood pressure regulation; sodium content (155 mg/100g) is moderate and within acceptable limits for hypertensive patients consuming a standard 2-slice (60g) serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low GI (~12) and very low glycaemic load (~0.6 per 2-slice serving); only 5.1g net carbohydrate per 60g serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Keto almond flour bread is an exceptional bread substitute for patients with Type 2 Diabetes: with a GI of approximately 12 and a glycaemic load of 0.6 per 2-slice serving, it produces minimal postprandial glucose excursion and can replace standard white bread (GI ~70, GL ~10 per 2 slices) with approximately 80% carbohydrate reduction, making it one of the most clinically effective bread alternatives available in Singapore.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High monounsaturated fat (MUFA ~18.2g/100g from almonds); vitamin E (8.5 mg/100g); low refined carbohydrate
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dominant fat in keto almond flour bread is monounsaturated oleic acid from almonds, which is associated with improved LDL-cholesterol profiles and reduced cardiovascular risk; vitamin E (8.5 mg/100g) provides antioxidant vascular protection, and the absence of refined carbohydrates avoids postprandial triglyceride elevation — making this a cardiovascularly favourable bread alternative for patients with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory MUFA and vitamin E from almonds; gluten-free formulation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Keto almond flour bread provides vitamin E (8.5 mg/100g — among the highest food sources), magnesium, and MUFA, all with anti-inflammatory properties; it is gluten-free, which is relevant for the subset of rheumatoid arthritis patients who also have coeliac disease or non-coeliac gluten sensitivity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Keto Bread (Almond Flour Bread) — known in Chinese as 生酮杏仁粉面包 (Xìngrén Fěn Miànbāo) — is a grain-free, gluten-free bread made primarily from blanched almond flour, eggs, butter, and baking powder, with no wheat flour or other grains. It is the most commonly requested healthy bread alternative from patients with Type 2 Diabetes and obesity in Singapore clinical settings. The bread looks and behaves like regular sandwich bread but contains approximately 80% less carbohydrate per slice: approximately 3g net carbohydrate per 30g slice versus 13–15g for standard white bread. The glycaemic index is approximately 12 (essentially non-glycaemic), making it one of the lowest-GI bread alternatives available. Texture is moist and slightly dense with a mild nutty flavour from the almond flour; it toasts well and can be used for sandwiches. Nutritionally, per 100g it provides 395 kcal, 12.5g protein, 35.5g fat (predominantly MUFA from almonds), 8.5g total carbohydrate, 4.5g dietary fibre, and 4.0g net carbohydrate. It is rich in vitamin E (8.5 mg), magnesium (65 mg), potassium (248 mg), and phosphorus (188 mg). In Singapore, it is available at Cold Storage (BFree, Delight keto brands), specialty keto bakeries (The Baking Affair, Keto SG), and online delivery services, and can be made at home in approximately 40 minutes. Important caveat: not all products labelled 'keto bread' in Singapore supermarkets are genuinely low-carbohydrate — some use wheat gluten or psyllium husk with added wheat starch. Consumers should verify that net carbohydrate per 30g slice is ≤3g for a genuinely keto or low-carbohydrate bread.
common_user_queries: is keto bread good for diabetics

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by weighted composite calculation from USDA SR Legacy component entries (almonds, eggs, butter, baking powder) using an approved methodology, as no single primary source entry exists for keto almond flour bread. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for almonds. Confidence is medium rather than high because the values are composite-derived rather than directly measured from a single approved source entry for this specific food.
notes: 1. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero — almonds, eggs, and butter contain no meaningful vitamin C; this is a naturally zero value for this food formulation, confirmed per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data. 2. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the baked composite product is not directly available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a single entry; detailed moisture data for this specific baked formulation would require laboratory analysis. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g (18.2g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (6.8g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy almond fatty acid profile weighted by ingredient proportion; these are not reported in the input record and have been added from approved source derivation. 4. Not all products labelled 'keto bread' in Singapore supermarkets are genuinely low-carbohydrate — consumers should verify net carbohydrate ≤3g per 30g slice. 5. FoodEx2 code A000E assigned as closest_parent only; no specific FoodEx2 code exists for almond flour or keto bread. 6. Halal status assumes no non-halal additives in the specific product; consumers should verify halal certification on packaged products.
